Episode 547: Interviewing Prospective Members

February 28, 2018 by Ivan Misner 5 Comments

Podcast: Download

Subscribe: Apple Podcasts | RSS | Subscription Options

Print This Episode

Synopsis

This episode goes hand in hand with Episode 541, “Inviting Visitors.” That episode explains that the best way to invite a prospective member to BNI is to say “I’m in a referral group. We’re interviewing people from your profession to find the best person from that profession that we can give all our business to.”

Once that person visits, you have to conduct an interview. Remember, this is an interview, not an interrogation. Keep it conversational, and choose just a few of these questions. Start with questions based on the BNI membership application.

  • How did you get to where you are in business?
  • How have you progressed over the years you’ve been in business?
  • Has your education helped you in business in some way?
  • Are you willing and able to arrive at the weekly meetings on time and stay for the 90 minutes?
  • What are your thoughts about our Code of Ethics?
  • Who would you send as a substitute if you’re not able to make it to a meeting?

Here are more questions, suggested by BNI members:

  • What about BNI appeals to you?
  • Where do you see your business going a year from now?
  • Where are you currently networking?
  • Why would BNI be good for your business?
  • How do you think you can help the group?
  • How is it the group can help you?
  • Would you be open to having a mentor work with you?
  • How would you implement the Givers Gain philosophy in your business?
  • Are you prepared for the success that can come with referrals?
  • Do you consider yourself a team player?
  • Of all your marketing efforts, what has the best ROI?
  • What percentage of your clients/customers/patients refer business to you now?
  • If you could wave a magic wand and grow your business in an incredible manner, what would that look like?
  • How important is referral marketing in your business today?

Don’t ask all these questions. Stop when you feel like you have enough information about whether the visitor is a good fit for your chapter. If you interview applicants like this consistently, you’ll build an incredibly successful chapter.

[gview file=”https://www.bnipodcast.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/02/Were-Interviewing-Sample-Questions.pdf”]

If you have a question that isn’t in the PDF, leave it here in the comments.

Brought to you by the Networking for Success Channel on YouTube.

Complete Transcript of Episode 547 –

Priscilla:

Hello everybody, and welcome back to the official BNI podcast brought to you by The Networking For Success channel on YouTube. Which features Dr. Ivan Misner and many other networking experts. I’m Priscilla Rice and I’m coming to you from LiveOak Recording Studio in Berkeley, California. I’m joined on the phone today by the founder and the chief visionary officer of BNI, Dr. Ivan Misner. Hello, Ivan! How are you?

Ivan:

I am doing fantastic! I am sitting in my library in Austin, looking over at Lake Austin, doing the podcast. So all is good!

Priscilla:

Well, good! What is today’s podcast about?

Ivan:

Well, this is a podcast that goes hand-in-hand with episode 541 “Inviting Visitors.” That was a classic podcast that we did earlier. And we broadcast it as episode 541, so after you are done listening to this, go back and listen to it. But right now, I’m going to give you the summary quickly. It is what I believe is the best technique to invite visitors to BNI:

I’m in a referral group and we get together regularly. We interview people from your profession to find the best person in that profession that we can give all of our business to. The people who I think would make a good candidate.

That’s it! Now what I talk about in there is, afterwards you actually have to do interviews. You can’t just say that you’re interviewing candidates and then take the first person you check. What I have never really done is spend some time writing out questions- I mean I do the interviews just off the top of my head, but I never wrote out potential questions. That’s what this podcast is about.

You use that technique to get people to visit, and then Woody as’s them in terms of interviews, “I want to give you simple questions…” Now, with this podcast, there will be a PDF that you can download that are sample questions – and I’m going to give you some of them now, but there will be a lot more in the PDF that you can download. Remember that these are sample questions, not an interrogation. Don’t put them in a small room with a big light. And don’t surround them with a bunch of BNI members and grill them on questions. It’s a conversation. You have to make it conversational. And don’t use all the questions on this list. Use enough questions to get a feel for the person’s commitment. That’s what you got to do. Also, do as many open ended questions as possible, not closed ended questions. Close ended questions are questions that end in a “yes” or “no.” For example, “will you do this?” And they respond “yes/no.” But instead ask “How will you go about doing this?” That’s a good open ended question and they can elaborate. Try to ask as many open ended questions as possible.

So, let’s begin.

So far, so good – hopefully everyone listening to this or reading this, will back to episode 541 to go back deep into the process and come to this podcast. This is for the questions. Let’s start with the membership application. I do mention this in the previous podcast. You can really do the whole interview just from the application by saying to them – and the application is very similar all around the world. It’s not identical but it’s very similar. It has these kinds of things based off of experience. Ask them “Tell me a little bit about your experience. How did you get to where you are in business?” That’s one of the questions on experience. The next one is length of time in that classification, “You’ve been doing this for *x amount of years* how has that been and how have you progressed?” Get them to open up and talk about it.

Don’t just ask them yes or no questions like, “Is it true that you have been doing this for 12 years?” That’s not what we are looking for. Now, educational background, I would ask something like, “How has your education/background helped you in business in some way?” Talk about that for a little bit. Another question on the application is, “Are you willing and able to arrive at weekly meetings on time and stay for the whole 90 minutes?” Now, that is a yes or no question, and I’m good with that because that is am important one to ask. “Are you willing to make this commitment?” And “Are you willing to follow the BNI policies and our code of ethics?” Now here’s an open ended spin on that: “What are your thoughts about our code of ethics and how do you feel about that?” Listen to what they have to say. They are probably going to say it’s great, but what you want is to get a feel for how committed they are.

For example, what if they said, “I think it’s awesome. I really think that the fact that the organization has a code of ethics, and appears to be concerned with that code of ethics, is what kind of organization I want to belong to.” That’s a great answer! Just listen to what they have to say.

One of the questions on the application is, “Are you willing or able to send a substitute if you can’t attend the meeting?” Well, that is a yes or no, but use the follow up, “Who do you think that might be? You don’t have to commit to who at this point, but who do you think that might possibly be?” It’s okay to lead them a little bit with that, “It could be a client, it could be an associate at your office, it could be your parter – whoever you may have. Who do you think it might be?” And it is okay if they don’t know. You just want to make sure that they get that it is important. They may say, “You know I don’t know who it really is, but I’ll find somebody. I’m willing to commit to that.”

Another question is, “Are you willing and able to bring referrals to this chapter/group–it doesn’t have to be right away, but over time?” That’s sort of a yes or no, but that’s okay. There’s a couple yes or no questions like that, but mostly open ended. You could do that whole interview just from that application, that’s what I always did. But wait, there’s more! I’m going to give you a whole series of questions- some of which were given to me on Facebook. I did some crowd sourcing and asked the members what questions that they might have. So I’m going to share as many as I can, but there will be a lot more on the PDF.

Gail had questions like, “What about BNI appeals to you?” I love that question!
“Where do you see your business going a year from now?”
“Where are you currently networking?”
“What do you like about those organizations?”
“What would you change?”

Anthony says, “Is this something you think would be good for your business? And why would this be good for your business?”

I think it’s really good sometimes to ask scale question like, “On a scale from 1-10, how important is something like BNI to you for generating referrals?” And they’ll give you a number and you ask why and get them to open up. “How do you think you can help the group? How is it that the group can help you?”

Mavis had so many good questions. Here’s a great one, “As a new member, would you be open as to having a mentor work with you in the chapter?” I think that’s a great question!

Here’s another one, “Our philosophy in BNI is ‘Givers gain.’ How would you implement this into your business and into BNI?”

“Do you see any conflicts with the policies and our system and your willingness to participate in it?” Mavis recommended this as a discussion for the referral confidence curve, “It may take 3-6 months or more, would this be a challenge for you? Depending on your business – just so you know this isn’t a get rich quick scheme, are you okay with that?” Asking the right questions is good at setting expectations, and setting expectations is so important with BNI.

Here’s one that several people suggested: “If you’re good at what you do, and you follow the system, you’re likely to have some success and get referrals. Are you prepared for the success that can come with referrals?”

“Are you in it to just get some referrals and then leave? We want people who want long-term commitment.” Get them to talk about that.

Here’s one from Myrah, “Do you consider yourself a team player? BNI is a team sport.”

James had one: “Of all your marketing efforts, what’s your best ROI in terms of marketing?”

Tim suggested, “What percentage of your clients, customers, or patients refer business to you now? Where would you like to go with that?”

Mike asks– and I like this concept– “If you could wave a magic wand and grow your business in a incredible manner, what would that look like?” That’s a great question to get them to open up!

This question is a variation of what I said from the application, “How do you see our chapter helping you? How do you see yourself contributing to others’ magic wand?”

Angelina asks, “If you are accepted to the meeting, do you think you could invite people into the network? How would it be for you to invite people in?”

And Jan asks, “How important in referral marketing in your business today?”

So these are a number of the questions, not all of them, that people asked or shared with me when I crowd sourced it on Facebook. I added some in there as well. Remember–and you will have a PDF of all of these questions and more–don’t ask them all. Don’t ask a person all of these questions because it’s too much. Pick and choose the ones that resonate with you and stop whenever you have enough information. It’s not like you have to check off every question. Now, when you feel that it’s a good fit, stop, you’re done!

And what I love about this is that it follows through with our recommendation of we’re interviewing for someone in your business, do the interview, lane your expectations, and your listening to their attitude. Remember it’s aptitude and attitude. So you’re not only looking at their aptitude, but you’re also listening to their attitude. And from that attitude, you may realize that it is not a good fit, or you may realize that it is a fantastic fit. You’ve laid up the expectations in terms of attending training and coming to the meeting.

I’m here to tell you, Priscilla, if chapters do this consistently you will have “knock-it-out-of-the park” success. And if there’s any non-baseball playing countries that may not understand that reference, it means a huge success!
They will have a huge success in building beautiful successful chapters! And I thought it was about time to actually provide questions that people could use.

What do you think, Priscilla?

Priscilla:

I think it’s what you said is important. That you could intimidate someone if you ask too many and it could come across as, “this may seem too demanding for me.”

Ivan:

Yeah. It’s the unintended consequence of a seemingly good idea. And it is a good idea, but the unintended consequences are if you make it too formal, they will feel intimidated. I wouldn’t say to them, “Oh we don’t want to intimidate you.” Because then just using the word intimidate is intimidating. Instead, simply say, “We would like to ask some questions because BNI is a team. It’s a bunch of people working together and we want to make sure it’s a good fit. So we want to ask you some questions- and there aren’t necessarily wrong or right answers, we just want to make sure that it’s a good fit with the team. It”s very conversational, so if you could respond, that would be great! And if you have any questions as I go along, fill free to ask them!” Make it conversational, make people feel comfortable, and get the best of the best.

And if you have a question that is not on my PDF or not covered in this podcast, I’d love for you to post it here on BNIpodcast.com. I read all your posts–I don’t always reply or respond to all of them, but I read them all, I promise.

That’s everything I got, Priscilla.

Priscilla:

That’s great! Thank you so much, Ivan!

Ivan:

My pleasure!

Priscilla:

Well, I would like to remind the listeners that this podcast has been brought to you by The Networking For Success channel on YouTube. Thank you so much for listening, this is Priscilla Rice. We look forward to having you join us again next week for another exciting episode of The Official BNI Podcast.

.

    Solutions-focused comments are welcome. Dr. Misner reads every comment personally.

    All comments are moderated before posting. Profanity, personal attacks, and sales pitches will be deleted.

  1. Paul English says

    February 28, 2018 at 1:39 pm

    Great session – some good education ideas. Great to have the pdf sheet

    Reply

  2. Ryan Babbit says

    March 8, 2018 at 10:47 am

    Take away; Aptitude and Attitude

    Keep the intimidation low by not asking to many questions, being to formal, excessive details, and make them feel comfortable.

    Thank you Ivan,

    Ryan Babbit

    Reply

  3. Joseph McCurry says

    April 27, 2018 at 6:39 pm

    As an ex Human Resources practitioner, I have been trained, and practised questioning as outlined in this podcast. I have listened to the podcast prior to interviewing a member, and have listened again this morning. Very helpful reminders for me. Recommended reading for members of membership committees.

    Reply

  4. Anthony says

    June 12, 2018 at 4:11 am

    Is there specific questions the membership committee should ask a member who is in trouble or up for renewal?

    Reply

  5. Noemi M says

    February 9, 2022 at 11:09 am

    Very nice and timely for me because Our MC will have our 1st time this week to conduct interview for an applicant. Thank.you for the tips. Excited and somewhat nervous to do my task being a newly launched chapter we are all exploring and learning a lot. Yes there is always the first time..

    Reply
